goal_11.config.title: Building productive cities for an urbanizing world
goal_11.config.abstract: Cities are the engine of global economic growth and offer greater economic opportunity, diversity in jobs, and better infrastructure. Yet, the benefits of urbanization are not automatic. As urban populations and cities continue to expand, strategic investment in urban planning will be essential to avoid slums and inequality and ensure cities deliver opportunity for all.
goal_11.config.homeFactoidText: of the world population lives in urban areas today
goal_11.config.homeTagline: Cities are the engine of global economic growth, but strategic investment in urban planning is essential to avoid slums and inequality
goal_11.content.#1.text: In just over six decades, the world has become predominantly urban. In 1960, just one billion people lived in cities. Today, that number has surged to 4.7 billion—about 58 percent of the world’s population.[reference: wup]
goal_11.content.#2.title: Urban and rural population by region (millions)
goal_11.content.#2.keyFactLabel: Of the world population live in urban areas today.
goal_11.content.#2.scenes.#0.text: But this transformation has not unfolded evenly across regions.
goal_11.content.#2.scenes.#1.text: Over the past 25 years, [emphasis: Europe] and [emphasis: Central Asia] have seen little overall population growth, with slight growth in urban populations and slight decline in rural populations.
goal_11.content.#2.scenes.#2.text: In [emphasis: North America] and[emphasis:  Latin America & the Caribbean], rural populations have plateaued while cities have continued to expand steadily.
goal_11.content.#2.scenes.#3.text: In [emphasis: East Asia & the Pacific], urbanization has been particularly dramatic: urban populations have grown rapidly as rural populations declined, with urban population overtaking rural in 2008. This shift was driven not only by migration or population growth but also by the expansion of city boundaries, absorbing once-rural areas into urban zones.
goal_11.content.#2.scenes.#4.text: Meanwhile, in [emphasis: Sub-Saharan Africa], [emphasis: the Middle East and North Africa, Afghanistan] [emphasis: and Pakistan], and [emphasis: South Asia], both rural and urban populations have grown. In Sub-Saharan Africa and in the Middle East and North Africa region, urban populations have expanded at a much faster pace than rural ones.
goal_11.content.#3.text: Urbanization is far from over. By 2050, South Asia is projected to be the only region where most people will still live in rural areas.[reference: wup] But the future of urban growth will look different from the past.
goal_11.content.#4.text: The next chapter of urbanization will not be defined by ever-larger megacities, but by the rapid spread of smaller urban centers across the globe.[reference: wup]
goal_11.content.#5.title: The world cities
goal_11.content.#5.subtitle: Vis subtitle
goal_11.content.#5.keyFactLabel: New cities will emerge in the next 25 years.
goal_11.content.#5.scenes.#1.text: In 2000, there were just over 9000 cities in the world.
goal_11.content.#5.scenes.#2.text: By 2025, the world had over 12,100 cities. Small and very small cities (those with fewer than 1 million residents) drove some of this growth, expanding from roughly 8,700 to 11,600. Meanwhile, megacities — those exceeding 10 million people — grew significantly as well, increasing from 20 in 2000 to 33 in 2025.
goal_11.content.#5.scenes.#3.text: However, in the next 25 years, the number of the world's largest cities is not expected to increase at the same pace. By 2050, projections suggest only 4 additional megacities will exist — bringing the total to 37.
goal_11.content.#5.scenes.#4.text: Instead, urban growth will be driven by an increase in the number of smaller cities. By 2050, their number is projected to approach 15,000.
goal_11.content.#6.text: Urbanization flows from three distinct channels: natural population growth within cities, rural-to-urban migration, and the reclassification of densely populated rural areas as urban.[reference: wb-gmr2013] While it is difficult to distinguish the extent of each channel at a global scale, there is some evidence that historically an increase in urban population happened because of rural-urban migration,[reference: wb1974]  but in the future, the increase in urban population will largely come from population growth.[reference: wb2021]
goal_11.content.#7.text: As agriculture becomes more productive and requires fewer workers, cities become the natural destination for those seeking better opportunities.[reference: jedwab2017] As workers move from lower-productivity rural jobs into higher-productivity urban industries, overall economic output rises.[reference: jedwab2017]
goal_11.content.#9.text: Far from being just a demographic shift, urbanization can be an economic opportunity. Across history, countries have grown richer as they have grown more urban.[reference: econgrowth] Why? Because cities create the conditions that make productivity and innovation thrive.[reference: blog-jobs]
goal_11.content.#10.text: That is why urban expansion often happens along with economic growth.
goal_11.content.#11.title: Urbanization is associated with economic growth
goal_11.content.#12.text: Urbanization enables productivity because it benefits both industries and people. As populations concentrate in cities, the forces that drive productivity—scale, specialization, and innovation—tend to strengthen.[reference: blog-jobs]
goal_11.content.#13.text: When firms cluster together, they can function more efficiently. Being close to suppliers, customers, and competitors lowers costs. Large, dense markets make it easier to find specialized workers and match their functions with skills. Proximity to other firms also fosters knowledge spillovers: ideas spread faster, which boosts innovation and growth. Urban hubs are key nodes in trade and transport networks, reducing shipping and coordination costs for goods and services. Better connectivity makes it easier for firms in cities to access global markets and value chains, which can further accelerate growth.[reference: quigley2008] This economic benefit from concentration of people and economic activities is called agglomeration effects.[reference: jedwab2017]
goal_11.content.#14.text: People benefit too. Cities offer a chance at economic mobility, more diversity in occupations, better pay, and better infrastructure.
goal_11.content.#16.text: In many developing countries, urban labor markets are often described as segmented into two broad tiers: a higher-wage formal sector and a lower-wage informal sector.[reference: wb2002] However, even low-wage informal jobs can offer pathways to build skills, gain experience, and move into better opportunities over time—especially in dense urban markets where transitions between jobs are easier.[reference: wb2021]
goal_11.content.#17.text: Let’s compare the “specialization of each skill” in urban and rural areas. The specialization indicator shown in the chart below compares the share of jobs for each skill level located in urban or rural areas with the share of all jobs located in urban or rural areas respectively. Each dot represents a country. If a particular skill-level job is more specialized in urban areas than employment overall, the indicator is above one for that country. When the value is closer to one, the jobs in this skill-level are distributed much like other jobs. Values below one suggest that jobs in this skill level are relatively less specialized in cities.
goal_11.content.#18.text: Low- and medium-skilled jobs are specialized in rural areas. In urban areas, however, their specialization levels hover around one, meaning these jobs are represented roughly in proportion to overall employment. By contrast, high-skill jobs are strongly represented in urban areas, with specialization rates far higher in urban areas than in rural regions—highlighting cities’ role as hubs for advanced work.
goal_11.content.#19.title: Urban areas offer jobs of a variety of skills
goal_11.content.#19.subtitle: Specialization of jobs by skill level across rural and urban areas
goal_11.content.#19.source: Source of the data Note: will add formula for specialization
goal_11.content.#20.text: Prosperity depends on how well countries attract productive workers, match them to the right jobs, and help them continue to build skills. Cities make this easier because skilled workers learn faster when they are surrounded by peers—people with similar or complementary skills, ideas, and experience. The result is higher productivity in urban areas, reinforcing cities as engines of economic growth.[reference: wdr2013]
goal_11.content.#22.text: This diversity in skill levels also means a wider range of occupations.
goal_11.content.#23.text: Urban areas widen the range of jobs people can do. By concentrating commerce, industry, transport, and services in one place, cities create far more varied labor demand than rural areas, where work is largely centered on agriculture.[reference: quigley2008] Cities can support both everyday occupations and highly specialized roles that rural economies cannot sustain. Larger cities, in particular, don’t just offer more of the same jobs—they reorganize work into more specialized and interconnected roles.[reference: task2002]
goal_11.content.#24.text: Across almost all occupations—except agriculture, forestry, and fishing—jobs are more specialized in urban areas. This highlights how cities offer a far wider and more diverse set of work opportunities for workers than rural areas.[reference: task2002]
goal_11.content.#25.title: Urban areas offer a variety of occupations
goal_11.content.#25.subtitle: Specialized skill indicator
goal_11.content.#25.visdescription: Across almost all occupations—except agriculture, forestry, and fishing—jobs are more specialized in urban areas. This highlights how cities offer a far wider and more diverse set of work opportunities for workers than rural areas.
goal_11.content.#25.label_OCU_ISCO08_8: Plant and machine operators, and assemblers
goal_11.content.#26.text: For many workers, urban labor markets also mean mobility. People rarely stay in one job for long; instead, they move between employers and sectors, and sometimes between informal self-employment and wage work. The density of opportunities in cities makes it easier to keep searching for a better match between skills, preferences, and pay.[reference: blog-jobs]
goal_11.content.#27.text: This diversity of occupations also acts as a form of insurance. When one industry slows, workers in cities can often shift into other sectors without having to move away. This makes urban economies more resilient and individual careers more adaptable.[reference: urban-sustainability] With more job options close by, people can switch roles, build new skills, and move up the income ladder without uprooting their lives.[reference: blog-li]
goal_11.content.#29.text: Jobs in cities tend to be higher skilled, formal, and more productive—and that usually means higher pay. Cities bring together larger and more diverse labor markets, with more firms competing for workers, especially in higher-skill sectors. This competition, along with better infrastructure and job matching, helps push wages up.
goal_11.content.#30.text: Of the 79 countries with data, average hourly wages are higher in urban areas than in rural areas in 66 countries (that is over 80 percent). For example, in Benin and Peru the average hourly wages in urban areas are 40 percent higher than rural areas.
goal_11.content.#31.title: Hourly earning of urban and rural population
goal_11.content.#31.keyFactLabel: Of countries have higher wages in urban areas compared to rural areas
goal_11.content.#32.text: Of course, city life is also more expensive. Rents are higher, and congestion and crowding come with real costs. So higher wages in urban areas largely only compensate for higher expenses associated with being in a city. But for many workers and firms, the benefits still outweigh the downsides. Higher pay, better job options, and more opportunities to learn and grow make urban areas attractive. The sheer variety cities offer—of people, services, and experiences—adds value too, helping compensate for the higher cost of living.
goal_11.content.#34.text: Cities are generally better equipped with infrastructure services like clean drinking water, safely managed sanitation, transport, electricity, healthcare, education etc. It forms the basis for a productive and good quality of life.
goal_11.content.#35.text: Expanding education systems in urban areas is easier and costs less than expanding it in rural areas. Returns to education are thus generally found to be higher in urban than rural areas. The effect of urbanization on education is generally positive, and empirical literature shows this correlation especially in Asia. Urban populations have more chance to reach hospitals, care centers and sanitation. Health care systems are also more developed, which may lead to better health performance than those in rural areas. Moreover, urban workers have better access to transport and to other facilities such as water, the Internet and electricity. Therefore, firms and workers may have higher productivity in urban than rural areas.
goal_11.content.#36.text: There is a need for infrastructure for cities to be productive. The ones that are productive are the ones that have invested in infrastructure. So, governments should plan cities ahead of urban population growth. Otherwise, cities can grow in undesirable ways leading to slums, overcrowding, low economic opportunity, among others.
goal_11.content.#38.text: Cities have the potential to boost productivity and incomes—but urbanization doesn’t automatically lead to development. Mixed-use, walkable, economically integrated, and transit-rich places define good urbanism in any city, anywhere on the planet.
goal_11.content.#40.text: This is Addis Ababa, the capital of Ethiopia. The total area where any space in the city is covered by buildings or other such construction of any use is called built up area. In 1975, Addis Ababa had x km2 of built up area. In the next 50 years, the city expanded tremendously with total built up area reaching x. At the same time population in addis also grew from just x million people living in addis to y.
goal_11.content.#41.title: Build up area of Addis Ababa over time
goal_11.content.#42.text: How a city manages such expansion determines its ability to do well, open opportunities for its residents, boost economic growth, and ensure it benefits everyone. A well-planned city preempts population growth.
goal_11.content.#43.text: If urban population growth happens rapidly without much planning, urban expansion can cause many issues such as slums.
goal_11.content.#44.text: Slums: A Sign of Unmet Demand
goal_11.content.#45.text: Rapid population growth without sufficient urban planning can lead to overcrowding, placing pressure on infrastructure, increasing congestion, and straining basic services. Formal housing markets tend to serve middle- and higher-income households, leaving poorer residents—especially migrants arriving from rural areas—with few options but to settle informally. The growth of slums reflects this imbalance: demand for land and housing rises faster than governments and markets can provide affordable homes.
goal_11.content.#46.text: Today, about [emphasis: 23% of the world’s urban population—roughly 1.8 billion people—live in slum conditions], with the challenge particularly acute in low-income countries where progress in reducing slum populations has slowed. Although the share of people living in slums has declined globally over the past decades, progress often stalls after an initial improvement. In [emphasis: Latin America and the Caribbean], for example, the slum population share fell steadily in the early 2000s but has hovered around [emphasis: 17–19% in recent years]. Similarly, in Sub-Saharan Africa, slum population fell from every 6 out of 10 urban residents living slums to about 5 out of 10 in 2020. But since 2020, the share of people living in slums is rising again.
goal_11.content.#47.title: Slum Population
goal_11.content.#47.y_title: Population living in slums (% of urban population)
goal_11.content.#48.text: Even as shares remain stable or even fall, this is concerning. In areas with high slum population, the total population is growing rapidly. Due to this population growth, the total number of people living in slums have increased. In just 4 years, global slum population grew from around 840 million people living in slums in 2018 to greater than a billion people living in slums by 2022.
goal_11.content.#49.title: Slum totals
goal_11.content.#49.square_represents: Every square represents 1 million people living in slums
goal_11.content.#50.text: Economic growth can help reduce slums by raising incomes and enabling greater investment in housing and infrastructure, but without careful urban planning and adequate housing supply, slums can persist even in growing economies.
goal_11.content.#52.text: As cities expand, much more land will need to be planned and serviced. Built-up areas are projected to grow by more than [emphasis: 50 percent over the next 25 years], largely in middle-income countries, making the way cities expand increasingly important.
goal_11.content.#53.title: Build up area by income groups
goal_11.content.#54.text: For cities to deliver on their productive potential, how they grow matters as much as how fast they grow. A fragmented city — one where housing, jobs, and services are disconnected, where infrastructure does not extend to where people actually live, and where institutions lack the capacity to manage expansion — undermines the very agglomeration benefits that make urban areas valuable in the first place.
goal_11.content.#55.text: When growth is poorly managed, the result is high living costs, sprawling informal settlements, and social exclusion, with productivity held back rather than amplified. The alternative is not to restrict urban growth, but to shape it. This means planning ahead of expansion rather than reacting to it: extending road networks and services before populations arrive, encouraging efficient densities that make infrastructure investment viable, and ensuring that land use connects people to opportunity. Cities in Sub-Saharan Africa, where urban populations are growing fastest, face this challenge most acutely — but also have the greatest opportunity to get it right, since much of their urban fabric is yet to be built.
goal_11.content.#56.text: Ensuring that new urban areas remain connected and well served will be critical for maintaining productivity. Productive cities depend on integrated labor markets where people can easily reach jobs. This requires promoting spatially connected development, encouraging sufficient density without overcrowding, and strengthening transport systems so workers can access opportunities within reasonable commute times. Careful planning today will determine whether expanding cities support opportunity and productivity in the future.
goal_11.references.keyOfReference: Here goes the content of the reference, which can include links like this. Add a reference in the text with [reference: keyOfReference]
goal_11.references.wb-gmr2013: World Bank — Global Monitoring Report 2013: Rural-Urban Dynamics and the MDGs (link)

goal_11.references.jedwab2017: Jedwab, R., Christiaensen, L., & Gindelsky, M. (2017) — "Rural push, urban pull and… urban push? New historical evidence from Africa"
goal_11.references.wb2021: World Bank — Migrants, Markets, and Mayors: Rising Above the Employment Challenge in Africa's Secondary Cities (2021) (link)
goal_11.references.econgrowth: World Bank. (2013/2014). Overview: The Urban Imperative — Toward Shared Prosperity. Washington, DC: World Bank. (link)
goal_11.references.blog-jobs: Zhang, Ming. (2025, March 10). "The world is facing a looming jobs crisis. Cities can help." World Bank Blogs. World Bank.
goal_11.references.Quigley2008: John M. Quigley.2008. Urbanization, agglomeration, and economic development. Washington, DC: World Bank.
goal_11.references.wb2002: World Bank. (2002). Policies for Efficient and Equitable Growth of Cities in Developing Countries. Washington, DC: World Bank. (link)
goal_11.references.wdr2013: World Bank. (2012). World Development Report 2013: Jobs. Washington, DC: World Bank. (link)
goal_11.references.task2002: World Bank. (2002). The Task Ahead for the Cities of the Developing Countries. Washington, DC: World Bank. (link)
goal_11.references.urban-sustainability: World Bank. (2018). Urban Sustainability Framework. Washington, DC: World Bank. (link)
goal_11.references.blog-li: Li, Y. and Rama, M. (2014). "Rural Jobs Allow People to Escape Poverty; Urban Jobs Are a Ticket to the Middle Class." World Bank Blogs. (link) — based on Addressing Inequality in South Asia, World Bank, 2014
